Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic this semester. I'd try to set aside time, maybe a couple hours here and there, but with everything going on it rarely stuck to a schedule. I'm more of a hands-on learner, so I'd often end up doing practice problems or working through examples physically rather than just reading. Most of my actual studying happened in bursts when I could manage my stress levels enough to focus.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and how I was feeling mentally. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything because the anxiety would get overwhelming, and then I'd have to cram closer to deadlines. I tried to be consistent but life kept throwing curveballs. When I did study, it was usually late at night when things felt calmer.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification that something new was posted. Sometimes my study group would message me about an assignment and I'd realize I needed to check what was going on. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—there had to be a specific reason pulling me there.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became automatic for me the way checking my phone is. There were maybe a few weeks where I'd check in more regularly when I was feeling better mentally, but it never stuck as a habit. The peer support group helped remind me to engage, which made a difference during certain stretches.
Q5: The video content was actually really helpful because I could pause and rewatch parts I didn't get the first time. I also found the discussion forums useful when my peers were active—seeing how others approached problems helped things click for me. Anything hands-on or interactive worked better for my learning style than just reading text.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the supplementary readings and some of the optional modules. When you're already stressed and short on time, anything that feels extra just gets pushed aside. I'd tell myself I'd get to it later but later never came. The stuff that wasn't directly tied to assignments or exams felt expendable.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I'd be tested on or if it helped me actually understand something I was struggling with. If I could physically work through a problem or apply a concept, that made it worthwhile. Anything that felt like busywork or just clicking through screens didn't hold my attention.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford most of the time. Required stuff had urgency attached to it, so even when I was anxious, I'd push through. Optional things got mentally filed under 'nice to have' and then forgotten. There's only so much energy when you're managing high stress and trying to just pass.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of materials and studied them on my tablet without being connected. I also used YouTube tutorials and external practice problem sites that explained things in different ways. My notes from peer study sessions were huge for me—we'd work through problems together on whiteboards and I'd photograph everything to review later.
Q10: There were many times I was genuinely learning but the LMS wouldn't know. Like when I'd spend hours with my study group working through concepts verbally and physically drawing things out. Or when I'd rewatch downloaded videos multiple times offline. My assignment scores were high because of that work, but my login frequency wouldn't show it.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download lecture videos and PDFs, then work through them over several days without logging back in. It was easier on my anxiety to study in my own space without feeling like I was being tracked or timed. I'd only go back online when I needed to submit something or check for updates.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when my anxiety spiked. I'd feel paralyzed and couldn't bring myself to engage even though I knew I was falling behind. The workload from all five online courses piled up and it felt impossible to catch up. Those were the weeks my attendance and engagement really dropped.
Q13: Hugely. When I was exhausted or overwhelmed, the LMS felt like this intimidating mountain I couldn't climb. Competing responsibilities—work, family stuff, other courses—meant something always had to give. Usually it was logging in regularly that suffered, even though I was still trying to learn through other means.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without anything sinking in. My mind would be racing with anxiety and I couldn't focus even though I was technically 'engaged.' I'd scroll through readings or let videos play while my thoughts were somewhere else entirely. Those sessions felt wasted.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they triggered my anxiety about being judged by peers. I knew participating mattered but putting my thoughts out there felt risky. I'd also avoid starting big assignments because the scope felt overwhelming—breaking them into smaller pieces helped but I had to learn that strategy the hard way.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system, for better or worse. I'd have these intense bursts of activity right before things were due. It's not ideal and it stressed me out more, but that pressure was sometimes the only thing that could cut through my anxiety paralysis. The LMS probably shows these spikes clearly.
Q17: Getting positive feedback was a huge boost—it helped quiet the anxious voice saying I wasn't good enough. When I saw that my assignment scores were strong, it gave me confidence to keep going even when engagement felt hard. Negative or no feedback made me withdraw more because I'd spiral into thinking I was failing.
Q18: The course structure was pretty flexible which was both good and bad for me. Good because I could work at my own pace during better mental health periods. Bad because without rigid structure, it was easy to fall behind when anxiety took over. Having peer support built in helped compensate for my inconsistent self-direction.
Q19: The video content being available for download made engagement way easier for me. What made it harder was when activities required real-time participation or had narrow submission windows—my schedule and mental state didn't always align with those. More asynchronous options would have helped someone like me engage more consistently.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is trying really hard but struggling to produce quality work, or when they're anxious and keep checking things without actually completing anything. It might also be someone who's learning but perfectionism keeps them from submitting. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience. My exam scores and assignment quality were decent because of all the offline work I did with peers and downloaded materials. The LMS only saw a fraction of my actual engagement. Someone looking at my login frequency might think I wasn't trying, but I was—just not in visible ways.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're active and what you click on, but they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if you're actually learning or just going through motions. They definitely miss offline studying, peer collaboration, and the mental and emotional effort that goes into managing yourself enough to even show up.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually mentally present and actively working to understand the material, not just completing tasks mechanically. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, connecting concepts to things I already know. Real engagement for me often happens in conversation with peers or when I'm physically working through problems—not necessarily when I'm logged into a platform.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I fought through anxiety to engage at all, or the hours I spent with my peer support group that kept me in the course. It wouldn't capture the resilience it took to keep going when I wanted to give up, or how my mood improved because of the connections I made. The human side of my learning journey is invisible to the data.
